caveats: a: BLS combines boat plants with much larger shipyards and therefore misstates the exact occupational mix.
a: Occupation shares are not task shares; leading-brand digital initiatives may run ahead of LMM builders.
rho: NIST covers manufacturers generally rather than boat builders.
rho: Brunswick is much larger and more technologically resourced than the target firms.
e: No source directly measures the share of LMM builders meeting the exact repeat-revenue and independence tests.
e: Large-company dealer networks and service models may not represent small custom builders.

s5: Stated plans do not ensure a completed qualifying sale or transfer.

q: Premium brands and custom builders may retain more than volume builders competing through multi-brand dealers.

d5: Brunswick is a diversified global leader whose brand mix, acquisitions, and dealer network differ from LMM builders.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat outsourced revenue through dealer and distributor programs, repeat production orders, replacement models, fleet or club purchases, accessories attributable to the manufacturer, warranty, and manufacturer-provided support.
lens.excluded_activities: Ship building and shipyard repair, floating-drydock repair, swimming-pool flotation devices, captive internal production, pure dealers or service yards, and manufacturers without recurring or repeat external-customer revenue.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of motorboats, fishing boats, pontoons, rowboats, canoes, kayaks, sailboats, yachts outside shipyards, heavy-duty inflatable boats, rigid inflatable boats, and boat-yard-built unmanned or robotic watercraft for external dealers, distributors, fleets, and end customers.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS employment for NAICS 336600, where production occupations were 46.83%, assemblers and fabricators 16.92%, fiberglass laminators 6.39%, engineering 9.70%, and installation and repair 6.21%. Transformation: mapped plausible exposed fractions of those occupations and supporting office work to a wage-weighted interval, emphasizing repeat-model design and production while excluding hands-on finishing and testing. Error direction: shipyard inclusion may understate standardized boat-production exposure, but bespoke yacht and small-batch builders may be less automatable than the base case.
caveats: BLS combines boat plants with much larger shipyards and therefore misstates the exact occupational mix.
Occupation shares are not task shares; leading-brand digital initiatives may run ahead of LMM builders.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from representative boat plants by hull material and production volume, explicitly separating already automated work, would materially move the range.
a.rationale: Exposure exists in styling and design iteration, quoting, forecasting, dealer support, documentation, scheduling, procurement, composite cutting and layup assistance, robotic fabrication, inspection, and inventory. Manual lamination, finishing, upholstery, assembly, wiring, water testing, customization, and accountable quality work remain difficult to substitute.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: NMMA estimates 2025 new-powerboat units declined 8% to 10% and expects 2026 sales to be roughly flat to slightly higher; Brunswick reports 2025 Boat segment volume down 4.6% and net sales down 1.8%. Transformation: used the current unit and manufacturer-volume contraction as the starting point, then allowed partial five-year normalization while discounting for financing, used inventory, dealer stocks, and shared access. Error direction: powerboat and large-brand data may overstate weakness in specialty or government craft, but a consumer downturn or persistent affordability pressure could make the base too high.
caveats: NMMA reports retail powerboats rather than all 336612 manufacturer shipments and does not provide a five-year forecast.
Brunswick is a diversified global leader whose brand mix, acquisitions, and dealer network differ from LMM builders.
d5.change_evidence: NMMA unit forecasts through 2031 by boat type, age and replacement of the installed fleet, dealer inventory, financing approvals, and shared-access fleet purchases would materially refine year-five real quantity.
d5.rationale: Participation, fishing, entry-level segments, replacement, and new technology support continuing boat demand, but the current market is weak. Elevated financing costs, discretionary-consumer sensitivity, dealer inventory, abundant used boats, tariffs, demographic shifts, and shared-access alternatives can keep new-unit quantity below earlier peaks.

caveats: No source directly measures the share of LMM builders meeting the exact repeat-revenue and independence tests.
Large-company dealer networks and service models may not represent small custom builders.
e.change_evidence: An NMMA member and Census establishment review linked to ownership, repeat dealer or fleet revenue, aftermarket attach, normalized EBITDA, and exact manufacturing scope would directly estimate eligibility.
e.rationale: Manufacturers with continuing dealer programs, repeat model production, fleet and club customers, replacement cycles, warranty, or manufacturer-linked accessories fit the lens. Eligibility falls for highly bespoke one-off builders, captive plants, pre-commercial ventures, pure dealers and repair yards, firms dominated by direct sporadic consumer sales, and businesses classified outside boat manufacturing.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336612 ($2,644,265,439) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336612 ($16,675,122,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $346,770 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336612); class <5: 351 firms, avg revenue/firm $566,336 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $51,197 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 137 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,812,496 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $163,850 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 109 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,543,422 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $229,925 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 146 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,148,630 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $827,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 78 firms, avg revenue/firm $67,538,949 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,105,521 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $389,460,625 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $35,207,240 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 78

caveats: Used boats and sharing mainly reduce new-unit demand and are therefore reflected in d5, with only residual role displacement in o.
This estimates persistence of the builder role rather than any brand or plant.
o.change_evidence: NMMA purchaser surveys and builder lost-sale data separating used-boat substitution, imports, kits, shared access, and manufacturer-free alternatives would materially move the surviving operator-required share.
o.rationale: New boats remain physical products requiring hull fabrication, systems integration, finishing, testing, product-liability accountability, and a manufacturer. AI, connectivity, autonomy, and electrification alter the product and process but do not eliminate the builder; a small share can be displaced by used boats, customer kits, imports, or shared fleets reducing new-unit needs.

caveats: This is discounted retained gross benefit rather than reported accounting margin.
Premium brands and custom builders may retain more than volume builders competing through multi-brand dealers.
q.change_evidence: Builder-level data on dealer agreements, wholesale pricing, rebates, model-year repricing, gross margin by model, productivity-sharing clauses, and post-improvement price realization would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Brand, design, dealer relationships, model differentiation, quality, and warranty can preserve some implemented benefit. Retention is constrained by transparent retail pricing, dealer margins and incentives, competing brands, model refreshes, promotions, floor-plan pressure, high price elasticity, and customers' ability to buy used boats or delay purchases.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years; Brunswick reports AI-enabled approaches and new technology in boat design and manufacturing. Transformation: translated those indicators into the five-year implemented share of exposed boat-building tasks, discounted for LMM capital, utilization, data, and validation constraints. Error direction: general intentions and a large-company example may overstate LMM realization, while falling automation costs and labor scarcity could make the central case conservative.
caveats: NIST covers manufacturers generally rather than boat builders.
Brunswick is much larger and more technologically resourced than the target firms.
rho.change_evidence: NIST MEP or NMMA plant-level case studies reporting completed deployments, cost, time to production, labor hours avoided, and quality outcomes by builder size would materially refine implementation.
rho.rationale: Repeat model lines, digital design, dealer data, and factory processes make exposed work implementable through AI, vision, analytics, CNC, and robotics. Smaller builders face capital constraints, legacy systems, weak data, seasonal utilization, product-liability validation, workforce skills, and change-management barriers.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup found 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years and 52.3% of employer businesses had owners age 55 or older. Transformation: used the intention rate as the central anchor and widened for strategic consolidation, execution failure, and distress. Error direction: owner plans likely overstate completed qualifying transfers, while unplanned strategic and health-triggered exits offset some of that shortfall.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries rather than boat builders or the target size band.
Stated plans do not ensure a completed qualifying sale or transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of boat-builder acquisitions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, marine brokers, and NMMA announcements, matched to owner age and normalized EBITDA, would materially refine the probability.
s5.rationale: Owner age and succession intentions support a material transfer pool, and consumer-product consolidation can create strategic exits. Qualifying transfers exclude closures, brand or asset sales without a viable operating company, minority financings, family continuity without control change, and distressed firms lacking transferable dealer relationships.

principal_driver: Repeat model platforms and dealer networks permit process automation and continuing replacement demand across a large recreational installed base.
principal_weakness: New boats are discretionary financed purchases with transparent alternatives, making volume and retained savings highly sensitive to consumer and dealer conditions.
